_meta.description: Per-CWE $ exposure estimates for vibe-coder triage. Ranges are conservative lower-bound estimates drawn from public incident data, not legal advice. Use for prioritisation only.

schema.best_case_usd: If exploited at small scale and noticed quickly
schema.likely_case_usd: Median outcome for a small SaaS / startup
schema.worst_case_usd: If exploited at scale before detection
schema.scenario: One-line plain-English description of the financial mechanism
schema.regulatory: Specific law / framework that triggers fines

CWE-89.name: SQL Injection
CWE-89.scenario: Full database read = every customer's data exfiltrated. PII leak → mandatory notification + regulator inquiry + potential class action.
CWE-89.regulatory: GDPR Art. 33 (72h notification), CCPA ($100-750 per CA resident), HIPAA ($50k-$1.5M per breach if health data)

CWE-78.name: OS Command Injection
CWE-78.scenario: Direct RCE — attacker pivots to your AWS metadata service, mints IAM creds, spins up crypto-mining fleet, exfiltrates DB, encrypts your S3.
CWE-78.regulatory: Same as CWE-89 plus potential SEC disclosure for publicly-traded customers

CWE-22.name: Path Traversal
CWE-22.scenario: Arbitrary file read leaks .env (= all your API keys = unlimited credential cost). Write variant lets attacker drop a webshell.
CWE-22.regulatory: GDPR Art. 33 if user files exposed

CWE-918.name: SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery)
CWE-918.scenario: SSRF to cloud metadata = stolen IAM credentials = uncapped cloud spend. Capital One: $190M settlement (2019 SSRF → metadata → 100M records).
CWE-918.regulatory: GLBA if financial data, GDPR/CCPA for PII

CWE-79.name: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
CWE-79.scenario: Session token theft for any user who loads the page. Persistent XSS = worm-style spread. Compounds with weak CSRF protection.

CWE-639.name: IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)
CWE-639.scenario: Enumerable IDs = mass PII exfil. T-Mobile API IDOR (2023) = 37M records exposed = ~$350M legal/cleanup costs.

CWE-352.name: CSRF
CWE-352.scenario: Action-on-behalf-of-victim for any state change. On payment endpoints = money movement. On email-change endpoints = account takeover gateway.
CWE-352.regulatory: GDPR Art. 32 (failure to implement appropriate technical measures)

CWE-915.name: Mass Assignment
CWE-915.scenario: Self-promotion to admin in one POST. Payment bypass via `{total: 0, status: 'paid'}`. Internal-flag injection (kyc_passed, email_verified).
CWE-915.regulatory: Payment-card-network fines and chargebacks if payment flow affected

CWE-287.name: Authentication Bypass
CWE-287.scenario: Forge JWT alg:none = impersonate any user including admin. Full account-takeover surface. Most enterprise contracts auto-cancel on this class of breach.

CWE-345.name: Missing signature verification (webhooks, JWTs, redirects)
CWE-345.scenario: Unsigned webhooks = attackers fake Stripe / GitHub / Clerk events to your endpoint = upgrade plans, mint tokens, trigger refunds.

CWE-502.name: Insecure Deserialization
CWE-502.scenario: RCE in the deserializer's process — same blast radius as CWE-78 plus often easier to exploit reliably (ysoserial / picklepwn / etc.).

CWE-1321.name: Prototype Pollution
CWE-1321.scenario: JS-only — DoS at min, RCE/auth-bypass in many real apps (Express, Mongoose, lodash gadgets).

CWE-798.name: Hardcoded Credential
CWE-798.scenario: API key in repo → scraped by bots within minutes. OpenAI/Anthropic keys: $40k overnight is common. AWS keys: crypto-mining costs $200k+ in hours.
CWE-798.regulatory: GDPR Art. 32; vendor T&C breaches voids your liability cap

CWE-601.name: Open Redirect
CWE-601.scenario: Phishing vector → credential theft. On OAuth callback = full token theft. Cache-poisoning amplifies the blast.
CWE-601.regulatory: GDPR if user creds are stolen

CWE-611.name: XXE (XML External Entity)
CWE-611.scenario: Arbitrary file read via XML parsing — .env, /etc/passwd, AWS metadata. SSRF via XML to internal services.

CWE-862.name: Missing Authorization
CWE-862.scenario: Endpoint accepts any caller — typical chain with IDOR for pre-auth account takeover. Optus (2022) class action settlement: $140M for missing-authz API.

LLM01.name: Prompt Injection
LLM01.scenario: Indirect injection via user-submitted doc → agent exfils secrets, calls expensive tools. No max_tokens cap + injection = unlimited LLM-API charges in hours.
LLM01.regulatory: Emerging — NIST AI 600-1, EU AI Act, state AI laws (CO, CA)

LLM02.name: Insecure LLM Output Handling
LLM02.scenario: LLM output → SQL / exec / browser → injection. Worse than user input because users trust the LLM.

LLM10.name: Unbounded LLM Consumption (no max_tokens, no rate limit)
LLM10.scenario: Prompt-injection attack on an uncapped endpoint = single bad day costs $20k-$100k in OpenAI/Anthropic bills. Air Canada chatbot incident: court ordered refunds.
LLM10.regulatory: Provider T&C limits, EU AI Act

DEFAULT.name: Generic finding
DEFAULT.scenario: Re-run with a recognised CWE for tailored exposure.
DEFAULT.regulatory: Depends on the data class exposed
